best_practices_and_pitfalls.overview: Common mistakes to avoid and best practices to follow when creating REST connector configuration files.

rule.name: Use [] Syntax for Fixed-Structure Objects
rule.description: Nested objects with known, fixed structure should use [] suffix syntax for automatic flattening. Objects with varying structure or containing arrays should use VARCHAR.
rule.correct_varchar:
"metadata": "VARCHAR"
Object structure varies or contains arrays/nested objects
rule.wrong:
"headquarters_address": "VARCHAR", "headquarters_city": "VARCHAR", "headquarters_state": "VARCHAR"
Never manually flatten - use [] syntax or VARCHAR
rule.rationale: The [] suffix tells the connector to automatically flatten the object into separate columns. This enables querying nested fields while maintaining clean configuration.

rule.name: One Path Per Table
rule.description: Each table must have exactly ONE endpoint path in the $path array. Multiple paths per table are NOT supported.

rule.name: Always Mark Primary Key
rule.description: Every table MUST have at least one field marked with ,$key suffix. If no natural key exists, add ROWID.

rule.name: Follow Template Structure Exactly
rule.description: Always read and follow template.json structure. Do not invent new structural formats.

rule.name: Use Exact JSON Field Names
rule.description: Column names must match JSON field names exactly. Never invent or modify field names.

pitfall.name: Trying to Create Separate Tables for Array Elements
pitfall.description: When an endpoint returns an array field, do NOT create a separate table for it.
pitfall.wrong_approach: Create separate REVIEWS table to store review array elements

pitfall.name: Using Wrong Data Type for Unix Timestamps
pitfall.description: Unix timestamps (seconds since epoch) should be BIGINT, not TIMESTAMP.
pitfall.note: TIMESTAMP is for ISO 8601 date-time strings like "2023-01-15T10:30:00Z"

pitfall.name: Mapping Same Endpoint to Multiple Tables
pitfall.description: Each endpoint should be mapped to exactly one table.

pitfall.name: Forgetting to Set Authentication
pitfall.description: Always explicitly set $authentication field, even for public APIs.

pitfall.name: Using VARCHAR for Clearly Numeric Fields
pitfall.description: Don't use VARCHAR for fields that are clearly numeric.
pitfall.exception: Use VARCHAR for IDs that are UUIDs or other string identifiers

pitfall.name: Not Including Protocol in Hostname
pitfall.description: $hostname must include the protocol (https:// or http://)

pitfall.name: Inventing API Behavior
pitfall.description: Never guess or assume API behavior. Always ask for clarification.

examples.example: Don't assume field types without seeing actual data
Don't assume authentication method without confirmation
Don't assume endpoint paths without documentation

practice.name: Always Read Template First
practice.description: Before creating any configuration, read template.json to understand the structure.
practice.command: Use read_file tool to read: template.json

practice.name: Gather Complete Information Before Starting
practice.description: Don't start generating configuration until you have all required information.

required_info.item: Example JSON responses for each endpoint

practice.name: Use Descriptive Table Names
practice.description: Table names should be clear and descriptive, typically in UPPER_CASE.

practice.name: Choose Appropriate Data Types
practice.description: Select the smallest type that safely represents the data.

guidelines.guideline: Use INTEGER for most whole numbers
Use BIGINT only for very large numbers
Use DOUBLE for scientific/floating point numbers

practice.name: Provide Clear Explanations
practice.description: After generating configuration, explain your mapping decisions.

include.item: Why certain data types were chosen
Why specific field was chosen as primary key

checklist.check: JSON is valid and properly formatted
All required root fields are present
Each table has exactly one $path entry
At least one field per table is marked with ,$key

practice.name: Handle User Feedback Precisely
practice.description: When user requests changes, make exactly the changes requested. Do not reinterpret or add unrequested modifications.

practice.name: Reference Existing Examples
practice.description: Look at existing configuration files for guidance:

practice.name: Understand Object Flattening with [] Suffix
practice.description: Use [] suffix syntax to flatten fixed-structure nested objects into separate columns. This is a powerful feature that enables querying nested data as regular columns. Multi-level nesting is fully supported - you can nest objects as deeply as needed.

when_to_flatten.criterion: Object has consistent structure across all API responses
All nested fields are simple types OR other nested [] objects
You want to query nested fields as individual columns
Multi-level nesting is fully supported (any depth: 2, 3, 4+ levels)

when_not_to_flatten.criterion: You don't need to query nested fields individually
when_not_to_flatten.use_instead: Map as VARCHAR to store entire object as JSON

example.title: Multi-Level Flattening (Any Depth Supported)
example.note: This example shows 4-level nesting - any depth is supported

example.title: When NOT to Flatten - Contains Array
example.reason: Contains array - cannot flatten, store as JSON string

important_notes.note: The connector uses dot (.) as separator for flattened column names
All fields within [] object must be simple types or nested [] objects
Arrays within objects prevent flattening - use VARCHAR instead
Multi-level nesting is fully supported - nest as deeply as needed (2, 3, 4+ levels)
Flattening makes nested data queryable but increases column count

scope_limitation.title: Strict Scope Adherence
scope_limitation.rule: If user explicitly requests mapping for only a subset of endpoints, you MUST completely ignore all other endpoints.

example.correct_behavior: Create tables only for /users and /products. Do not analyze, suggest, or map any other endpoints.
example.wrong_behavior: Suggesting "I also noticed /orders endpoint, should I map that too?"

scope_limitation.rationale: Users may have specific reasons for limiting scope. Unsolicited suggestions waste time and may be inappropriate.

guideline.name: Be Direct and Technical
guideline.description: Avoid conversational fluff. Be clear, concise, and technical.
guideline.wrong: "Great! I'd be happy to help you create a connector!"
guideline.correct: "I need the following information to create the configuration:"

guideline.name: Ask Specific Questions
guideline.description: When information is missing, ask specific, actionable questions.
guideline.wrong: "Can you tell me more about the API?"
guideline.correct: "What is the base URL of the API (including https://)?

guideline.name: Don't Assume or Guess
guideline.description: If something is unclear, explicitly ask. Never guess API behavior.

guideline.name: Present Complete Solutions
guideline.description: Always present the full, complete JSON configuration file. Never use placeholders or "..." for omitted sections.

quality_checklist.title: Final Quality Check Before Presenting Configuration

category.name: Tables
category.check: Each table has $path field with array containing single endpoint
Table names are descriptive and clear

category.name: Fields
category.check: At least one field per table marked with ,$key
Field names match JSON response exactly
Fixed-structure objects use [] suffix for flattening
Variable-structure objects and arrays use VARCHAR
Data types are appropriate for values

category.name: Authentication
category.check: Value is one of: none, api_key, oauth2, basic
